item.title: Good, bhikkhus. I too have not seen nor heard, bhikkhus, that an ascetic or brahmin, dwelling devoted to the enjoyment of the pleasure of resting,…
item.description:
Good, bhikkhus. I too have not seen nor heard, bhikkhus, that an ascetic or brahmin, dwelling devoted to the enjoyment of the pleasure of resting, the pleasure of lying down, the pleasure of sleeping, without guarding the sense doors, without moderation in eating, without devotion to wakefulness, without regard for wholesome states, without diligently developing the factors of enlightenment in the evening and in the morning, could, by the destruction of the taints, in this very life, with direct knowledge, realize, attain, and abide in the taintless liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom. Therefore, bhikkhus, you should train yourselves thus: 'With guarded sense doors, with moderation in eating, devoted to wakefulness, regarding wholesome states, diligently developing the factors of enlightenment in the evening and in the morning, we will, by the destruction of the taints, in this very life, with direct knowledge, realize, attain, and abide in the taintless liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom.' Thus, bhikkhus, you should train yourselves.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 6.17
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Feet painted with lac and ointment, Face powdered with white powder, Enough to fool a fool, But not one seeking the far shore.
item.description:
Feet painted with lac and ointment,
Face powdered with white powder,
Enough to fool a fool,
But not one seeking the far shore.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 82 – Raṭṭhapālasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: If an elder bhikkhu, Kassapa, delights in the training, praises undertaking the training, and encourages those bhikkhus who do not delight in the training, and…
item.description:
If an elder bhikkhu, Kassapa, delights in the training, praises undertaking the training, and encourages those bhikkhus who do not delight in the training, and genuinely and timely praises those bhikkhus who delight in the training — such an elder bhikkhu, Kassapa, I praise. Why is that? Because other bhikkhus might associate with that elder bhikkhu, thinking: 'The Teacher praises him.' And those who associate with him might adopt his views. And those who adopt his views would bring about their own long-term welfare and happiness. Therefore, Kassapa, I praise such an elder bhikkhu. If a middle bhikkhu, Kassapa ... If a junior bhikkhu, Kassapa, delights in the training, praises undertaking the training, and encourages those bhikkhus who do not delight in the training, and genuinely and timely praises those bhikkhus who delight in the training — such a junior bhikkhu, Kassapa, I praise. Why is that? Because other bhikkhus might associate with that junior bhikkhu, thinking: 'The Teacher praises him.' And those who associate with him might adopt his views. And those who adopt his views would bring about their own long-term welfare and happiness. Therefore, Kassapa, I praise such a junior bhikkhu.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.91
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Craving, bhikkhus, is rot; hatred is foul smell; evil unwholesome thoughts are flies. Bhikkhus, if one lets oneself rot, becoming saturated, emitting a foul smell,…
item.description:
Craving, bhikkhus, is rot; hatred is foul smell; evil unwholesome thoughts are flies. Bhikkhus, if one lets oneself rot, becoming saturated, emitting a foul smell, and then wishes: 'Flies will not swarm and settle on me,' such a thing is impossible.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.128
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Whatever beings, bhikkhus, whether footless or two-footed, four-footed or many-footed, whether having form or formless, whether percipient or non-percipient or neither-percipient-nor-non-percipient — the Tathāgata, the…
item.description:
Whatever beings, bhikkhus, whether footless or two-footed, four-footed or many-footed, whether having form or formless, whether percipient or non-percipient or neither-percipient-nor-non-percipient — the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One is declared the foremost among them. Those who have confidence in the Buddha have confidence in the foremost. For those who have confidence in the foremost, the result is foremost.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 4.34
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Bhāradvāja: Like lions in a mountain cave roaring the lion's roar, So with wisdom, the hero, victorious over Māra and his army. I serve…
item.description:
Elder Bhāradvāja:

Like lions in a mountain cave roaring the lion's roar,
So with wisdom, the hero, victorious over Māra and his army.

I serve the Teacher, honor the Dhamma and the Saṅgha,
I rejoice happily seeing the taintless one.

From: Theragāthā 2.29
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Whereas some samaṇas and brahmins, while enjoying food offered by the faithful, still live harming seeds and plants — such as root-born seeds, stem-born seeds,…
item.description:
Whereas some samaṇas and brahmins, while enjoying food offered by the faithful, still live harming seeds and plants — such as root-born seeds, stem-born seeds, joint-born seeds, cutting-born seeds, and fifthly, seed-born seeds — Samaṇa Gotama abstains from harming any seeds or plants. — Bhikkhus, these are the praises of the Tathāgata by ordinary people.

From: Dīgha Nikāya 1 – Brahmajālasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: I call one at peace who does not long for sensual pleasures. He has no bonds, having transcended attachment. He has no children, cattle, fields,…
item.description:
I call one at peace who does not long for sensual pleasures.
He has no bonds, having transcended attachment.

He has no children, cattle, fields, or land.
Nothing is grasped as 'mine' or 'not mine'.

Whatever worldly people or ascetics and brahmins might reproach him with,
He is not concerned; his mind is not shaken.

Without greed, without stinginess, the sage does not speak of superiority,
Nor of equality, nor of inferiority. He does not fall into time,
For he has transcended time.

One who has nothing, not having, does not grieve.
Not going to (phenomena) — that one is called at peace.

From: Sutta Nipāta 4.10
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - Venerable Sir, by knowing and seeing in what way, in this conscious body and in all external signs, the mind has become free from…
item.description:
- Venerable Sir, by knowing and seeing in what way, in this conscious body and in all external signs, the mind has become free from I-making, mine-making, and conceit, has transcended all conceit, peaceful, and liberated?
- Rāhula, whatever form, whether past, future, or present ... whatever feeling ... whatever perception ... whatever formations ... whatever consciousness, whether past, future, or present, internal or external, gross or subtle, inferior or superior, far or near — he sees all consciousness as it actually is with right wisdom: 'This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.' Seeing thus with right wisdom, he is liberated by non-clinging.
Rāhula, by knowing and seeing thus, in this conscious body and in all external signs, the mind has become free from I-making, mine-making, and conceit, has transcended all conceit, peaceful, and liberated.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 18.22
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: And, bhikkhus, what is a person with expansive wisdom? Here, bhikkhus, a person goes to the monastery and often hears the Dhamma from the bhikkhus.…
item.description:
And, bhikkhus, what is a person with expansive wisdom? Here, bhikkhus, a person goes to the monastery and often hears the Dhamma from the bhikkhus. The bhikkhus teach him the Dhamma, beautiful in the beginning, beautiful in the middle, beautiful in the end, with meaning and phrasing, revealing the completely perfected and purified holy life. While sitting there, he attends to the beginning of that discourse, attends to the middle, attends to the end. Likewise, when he rises from that seat, he attends to the beginning, attends to the middle, attends to the end. Just as, bhikkhus, an upright pot retains the water poured into it and does not pour it out, so too, bhikkhus, here a person goes to the monastery... attends to the end. Likewise, when he rises from that seat, he attends to the beginning... attends to the end. This, bhikkhus, is called a person with expansive wisdom.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.30
📖 Read this sutta
